replies.author: @anonnyanonymous4800
replies.text:
@erichwagner6958 it is that high. Theaters take a cut of the box office. You can (roughly) half the box office total to figure out whether a film has been profitable. With production costs being $200-$400 million (with tons of reshoots, having to redo CGI over and over, cost overruns, etc.) and marketing of $200 million, just getting to a box office of $600 million isn’t enough to make a film break even. The theaters are taking a cut of that $600 million. 
And in international markets, the theater cut is even higher than 50%. I believe it’s at 70%. And the longer a movie is showing in theaters, the theater share increases. Like with Titanic, where they showed it for months and months, after a certain number of weeks, the box office share of theaters would go up to 70% domestically. Maybe even higher.

replies.author: @davidgantenbein9362
replies.text:
Not necessarily. Big tentpole Disney movies used to do such numbers easily. Furthermore, there is money to be earned after cinema (dvd, digital sales, tv and streaming). In addition, merchandise plays a huge part in this as well. And last but not least, some Disney brands are directly important for the parks, which earn btw. most of the money at Disney.
You see, if a movie can attract a large enough audience to make the after cinema market superbly lucrative, it’s not that important that it only broke even at the box office. So for Disney’s classic business model, the most important thing was to attract huge amounts of people to set up decades of earnings from a brand, while not directly losing money at the box office.
That’s absolutely a sound strategy as long as you have something to sell that people want. Disney has a giant marketing department and a diverse and global range of offerings to harvest a brand. That’s why a brand’s positive reception is much more important than earning money at the box office. Hence Disney can afford a model, where it barely breaks even at the box office as long as the brand hits with the target audience and possesses the staying power to go for years to come (better if it’s actually decades given investment times on parks).

replies.author: @deco2gogo
replies.text:
Snow White came out the year my father was born, it's not exactly ancient. The Brothers Grimm included the story in their 1812 collection of German fairy tales, but it is certainly much older. Some theories compare the story to the Roman legend of Chione, from Ovid's Metamorphoses. The name Chione even means "Snow" in Greek. Some historians believe that Margaretha von Waldeck, (born in the 1500s), daughter of Philip IV, Count of Waldeck-Wildungen, who also had a jealous, evil step-mother, and her story may also have inspired that of Snow White.
What gets me about Zegler is her shameless lack of humility and her snarky implication that anything created before her time is sure to be sexist and racist by default. Actors and actresses of the Golden Age of cinema used to behave with poise, wit and charm, qualities which the narcissistic Zegler will  never achieve. She only knows how to repeat lame, woke talking points about the greatness of feminism and the evils of toxic white men. Her vile behavior has offended the few remaining Disney fans who may have considered forking over money to actually visit to a theater to personally witness this pile of manure. Lol.

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text:
You realize $5 was worth a lot more back then right? When I was a kid minimum wage was like $5 an hour. And that was not that long ago. My grandfather made like $11 a day IIRC, and he had a great job, a head engineer at a GE plant. People could easily afford $25 tickets if they were worth watching. But there are too many alternatives now. 
On the other hand, operating expenses have gone up with everything else, it's not cheap to run and insure a theater, and the fewer customers who go the more you have to charge each one to cover your expenses, after a certain point it creates a death spiral. But if a theater only had to try the bright idea of cutting prices in half and suddenly crowds would be flocking their doors, I feel like someone would have tried that already. They raised the prices because no one was coming already and they had no choice, and most of them are barely scraping by as it is. Personally I think that the theater industry is probably mostly doomed. We might have a few left over as quaint attractions, like we still have a couple drive-in theaters which are attractions in their own right, who just barely manage to get by by marketing themselves as a  unique experience and nostalgic trip.
